questionText: You are to get a patient with right-sided weakness up in a chair. On what side of the bed will you place the chair?
options: On the patient's left side
On the patient's weak side
Either side, because you are assisting the patient
On whichever side the patient prefers
correctAnswer: On the patient's left side

questionText: Which of the following patient should be allowed to lie back down?
options: A patient who was just transferred to a chair and states that she was more comfortable in bed. Physician's orders are to be up in the chair twice daily.
A patient whose blood pressure was 110/70 prior to transfer and is now 125/80.
A patient whose blood pressure was 120/80 prior to transfer, and is now 112/78.
A patient who complains of feeling dizzy and slightly nauseous when dangling on the bedside.
correctAnswer: A patient who complains of feeling dizzy and slightly nauseous when dangling on the bedside.

questionText: A nursing instructor asks you what may cause orthostatic hypotension. You correctly reply
options: Low body weight
Hyperkalemia
Room temperature
Prolonged bed rest
correctAnswer: Prolonged bed rest

questionText: Which of the following patients would require repositioning?
options: A patient who has been sitting in a chair for 10 minutes watching television
A patient in correct body alignment who was turned 2 hours ago
A patient with paraplegia who has been sitting in a chair for 10 minutes but states that she is comfortable
A patient who was repositioned for comfort 30 minutes ago after being moved up in bed
correctAnswer: A patient in correct body alignment who was turned 2 hours ago

questionText: Which of the following instructions provided by the nurse to ancillary staff assigned the responsibility of bathing a patient who has been prescribed elastic stockings is most likely to ensure the continued skin integrity of the patient's legs?
options: Be sure to tell me if you see any redness, dryness, or cracking on her legs.
Please let me know when the stockings are off so I can look at her legs.
Turning the feet of the stockings inside out will make putting them on easier.
Remember to apply a little powder to her legs before reapplying the stockings
correctAnswer: Please let me know when the stockings are off so I can look at her legs.

questionText: The nurse discovers a patient on the floor. The patient states that he fell out of bed. The nurse assesses the patient and places the patient back in bed. Which action should the nurse take next?
options: Do nothing, no harm has occurred
Notify the health care provider
Complete an incident report
Notify the charge nurse
correctAnswer: Notify the health care provider

questionText: A patient has an ankle restraint applied. Upon assessment the nurse finds the toes a light blue color. Which action will the nurse take next?
options: Remove the restraint
Place a blanket over the feet
Immediately do a complete head-to-toe neurologic assessment
Take the patient's blood pressure, pulse, temperature, and respiratory rate
correctAnswer: Remove the restraint

questionText: The nurse enters the patient's room and notices a small fire in the headlight above the patient's bed. In which order will the nurse perform the steps, beginning with the first one?
options: Remove the patient -> Pull the alarm -> Close doors and windows -> Use the fire extinguisher
correctAnswer: Remove the patient -> Pull the alarm -> Close doors and windows -> Use the fire extinguisher

questionText: A nurse is preparing to provide hygiene care. Which principle should the nurse consider when planning hygiene care?
options: Hygiene care is always routine and expected
During hygiene care, do not take the time to learn about patient needs
It is important to standardize a patient's hygienic practices.
No two individuals perform hygiene in the same manner
correctAnswer: No two individuals perform hygiene in the same manner

questionText: The nurse is providing oral care to an unconscious patient. Which action should the nurse take?
options: Moisten the mouth using lemon-glycerin sponges
Hold the patient's mouth open with gloved fingers
Suction the oral cavity
Use foam swabs to help remove plaque
correctAnswer: Suction the oral cavity

questionText: The nurse is providing perineal care to an uncircumcised male patient. Which action will the nurse take?
options: Leave the foreskin alone because there is little chance of infection
Retract the foreskin for cleansing and allow it to return on its own
Leave the foreskin retracted
Retract the foreskin and return it to it natural position when done
correctAnswer: Retract the foreskin and return it to it natural position when done

questionText: When providing hygiene for an older-adult patient, the nurse closely assesses the skin. What is the rational for the nurse's action?
options: Outer skin layer becomes more resilient
Skin becomes less subject to bruising
Less frequent bathing may be required
Sweat glands become more active
correctAnswer: Less frequent bathing may be required

questionText: To reduce the risk of aspiration how long should the nurse keep the bed up 30 to 45 degrees after eating?
options: 10 minutes
20 minutes
30 minutes
60 minutes
correctAnswer: 60 minutes

questionText: The patient is on parenteral nutrition and is lethargic. The patient reports thirst and headache and has had increased urination. Which problem does the nurse prepare to address?
options: hyperglycemia, high blood sugar, high blood glucose
correctAnswer: hyperglycemia, high blood sugar, high blood glucose

questionText: The nurse is preparing to insert a nasogastric tube in a patient who is semiconscious. To determine the length of the tube needed to be inserted, how should the nurse measure the tube? Place the landmarks in order.
options: Nose -> Ear -> Xyphoid process
correctAnswer: Nose -> Ear -> Xyphoid process

questionText: The patient has just started on enteral feedings and is reporting abdominal cramping. Which action will the nurse take next?
options: Stop the feeding
Slow the rate of the feeding
Change to a new formula
Reassure the patient it is normal
correctAnswer: Slow the rate of the feeding

questionText: What temperature do you administer an intermittent gastric tube feeding to the patient?
options: Chilled from the unit refrigerator
Warmed to 98-100 degrees Fahrenheit
Room temperature
correctAnswer: Room temperature

questionText: The patient is on parenteral nutrition and is diaphoretic, shaky, and confused. Which problem does the nurse prepare to address?
options: hypoglycemia / low blood sugar
correctAnswer: hypoglycemia / low blood sugar

questionText: A small-bore feeding tube is placed. Which technique will the nurse use to best verify the initial tube placement?
options: Inject air into the tube and auscultate over the abdomen
Aspirate gastric contents and check for pH
Obtain an order for an x-ray of the chest/abdomen
correctAnswer: Obtain an order for an x-ray of the chest/abdomen

questionText: A patient is receiving TPN.  What is the primary intervention the nurse should implement to prevent a central line infection? (Select all that apply)
options: Clean the central line port through which the TPN is infusing with an antiseptic; Change the TPN tubing every 24 hours
correctAnswer: Clean the central line port through which the TPN is infusing with an antiseptic; Change the TPN tubing every 24 hours

questionText: Which of the following interventions help to prevent aspiration in patients with dysphagia? (Select all that apply).
options: Allow plenty of time to eat meals; Offer thickened liquids as ordered; Coach the patient to use the chin-tuck technique when eating
correctAnswer: Allow plenty of time to eat meals; Offer thickened liquids as ordered; Coach the patient to use the chin-tuck technique when eating

questionText: An older adult is admitted to the hospital for multiple health problems. Assessment reveals that the client has no teeth and is having difficulty eating. Which diet should the nurse encourage the physician to order for this client?
options: Liquid supplements
Mechanical soft
Pureed
Soft
correctAnswer: Mechanical soft

questionText: The nurse evaluates which lab value to assess a patient's potential for wound healing?
options: Potassium
Lipids
Protein
WBC
correctAnswer: Protein

questionText: The nurse is caring for a patient with dysphagia and is feeding her a pureed chicken diet when she begins to choke. What is the priority nursing intervention?
options: Suction her mouth and throat
Turn her on her side
Put on oxygen at 2L/min via nasal cannula
Stop feeding her and place her on NPO
correctAnswer: Stop feeding her and place her on NPO

questionText: The nurse is caring for a client who will require enteral feedings for the next 8 weeks. Which type of line/tube is appropriate for this client (select all that apply)?
options: NG tube; G-tube
PICC line
Central Venous Catheter
correctAnswer: NG tube; G-tube

questionText: A client is on a clear liquid diet. Which of the following should the nurse teach the client to avoid while on this diet?
options: Strawberry Gelatin (Jell-o)
Plain decaffeinated tea
Black coffee
Pureed Soup
Chicken Broth
correctAnswer: Pureed Soup

questionText: A nurse is caring for a patient with chronic pain. Which statement by the nurse indicates an understanding of pain management?
options: This patient says the pain is a 5 but is not acting like it. I am not going to give any pain medication.
I need to reassess the patient’s pain 1 hour after administering oral pain medication.
It wasn’t time for the patient’s medication, so when it was requested, I gave a placebo.
The patient is sleeping, so I pushed the PCA button.
correctAnswer: I need to reassess the patient’s pain 1 hour after administering oral pain medication.

questionText: The nurse is caring for a patient who suddenly experiences chest pain. What is the nurse’s first priority?
options: Call the rapid response team.
Start an intravenous (IV) line.
Administer pain-relief medications.
Ask the patient to rate and describe the pain.
correctAnswer: Ask the patient to rate and describe the pain.

questionText: A nurse is caring for a patient with rheumatoid arthritis who is now going to be taking 2 acetaminophen (Tylenol) tablets every 6 hours to control pain. Which part of the patient’s social history is the nurse most concerned about?
options: Patient drinks 1 to 2 glasses of wine every night.
Patient smokes 2 packs of cigarettes a day.
Patient occasionally uses marijuana.
Patient takes anti-anxiety medications.
correctAnswer: Patient drinks 1 to 2 glasses of wine every night.

questionText: A patient is receiving opioid medication through an epidural infusion. Which action will the nurse take?
options: Restrict fluid intake.
Label the tubing that leads to the epidural catheter.
Apply a gauze dressing to the epidural catheter insertion site.
Ask the nursing assistive personnel to check on the patient at least once every 2 hours.
correctAnswer: Label the tubing that leads to the epidural catheter.

questionText: The nurse is assessing a patient for opioid tolerance. Which finding supports the nurse’s assessment?
options: The patient needed a substantial dose of naloxone (Narcan).
The patient needs increasingly higher doses of opioid to control pain.
The patient no longer experiences sedation from the usual dose of opioid.
The patient asks for pain medication close to the time it is due around the clock.
correctAnswer: The patient needs increasingly higher doses of opioid to control pain.

questionText: The nurse is caring for a patient to ease modifiable factors that contribute to pain. Which areas did the nurse focus on with this patient?
options: Age and gender
Anxiety and fear
Culture and ethnicity
Previous pain experiences and cognitive abilities
correctAnswer: Anxiety and fear

questionText: The nurse is teaching a student nurse about pain assessment scales. Which statement by the student indicates effective teaching?
options: You cannot use a pain scale to compare the pain of my patient with the pain of your patient.
When patients say they don’t need pain medication, they aren’t in pain.
A patient’s behavior is more reliable than the patient’s report of pain.
Pain assessment scales determine the quality of a patient’s pain.
correctAnswer: You cannot use a pain scale to compare the pain of my patient with the pain of your patient.

questionText: A nurse is providing discharge teaching for a patient with a fractured humerus. The patient is going home with hydrocodone. Which important patient education does the nurse provide?
options: You need to drink plenty of fluids and eat a diet high in fiber.
Narcotics can be addictive, so do not take them unless you are in severe pain.
Be sure to eat a meal high in fat before taking the medication, to avoid a stomach ulcer.
As your pain severity lessens, you will begin to give yourself once-daily intramuscular injections.
correctAnswer: You need to drink plenty of fluids and eat a diet high in fiber.

questionText: The nurse is caring for a group of patients. Which task may the nurse delegate to the nursing assistive personnel (NAP)?
options: Administer a back massage to a patient with pain.
Assessment of pain for a patient reporting abdominal pain.
Administer patient-controlled analgesia for a postoperative patient.
Inappropriate medical tests are the most common mistakes.
correctAnswer: Administer a back massage to a patient with pain.

questionText: A nurse is caring for a patient who recently had spinal surgery. The nurse knows that patients usually experience acute pain following this type of surgery. The patient refuses to get up and walk and is not moving around in the bed. However, the patient is stoic and denies experiencing pain at this time. What most likely explains this patient’s behavior?
options: The surgery successfully cured the patient’s pain.
The patient’s culture is possibly influencing the patient’s experience of pain.
The primary health care provider did not prescribe the correct amount of medication.
The nurse is allowing personal beliefs about pain to influence pain management at this time.
correctAnswer: The patient’s culture is possibly influencing the patient’s experience of pain.

questionText: A patient who has had type 2 diabetes for 26 years is beginning to experience peripheral neuropathy in the feet and lower leg. The nurse is providing education to the patient to prevent injury to the feet by wearing shoes or slippers when walking. Which statement made by the nurse best explains the rationale for this instruction?
options: Wearing shoes blocks pain perception and helps you adapt to pain, which ends up protecting your feet.
Shoes provide nonpharmacological pain relief to people with diabetes and peripheral neuropathy.
The neurological gates open when wearing shoes, which protects your feet.
If you step on something without shoes, you might not feel it; this could possibly cause injury to your foot.
correctAnswer: If you step on something without shoes, you might not feel it; this could possibly cause injury to your foot.

questionText: A nurse is caring for a patient who fell on the ice and has connective tissue damage in the wrist and hand. The patient describes the pain as throbbing. Which type of pain does the nurse document in this patient’s medical record?
options: Visceral Pain
Somatic Pain
Centrally Generated Pain
Peripherally Generated Pain
correctAnswer: Somatic Pain

questionText: The nurse is caring for a 4-year-old child who has pain. Which technique will the nurse use to best assess pain in this child?
options: Use the FACES scale.
Check to see what previous nurses have charted.
Ask the parents if they think their child is in pain.
Have the child rate the level of pain on a 0 to 10 pain scale.
correctAnswer: Use the FACES scale.

questionText: A nurse is caring for a group of patients. Which patient will the nurse see first?
options: A patient who received morphine and has a pulse of 62 beats/min, respirations 10 breaths/min, and blood pressure 110/60 mm Hg
A patient lying very still in bed who reports no pain but is pale with warm, dry skin
A patient with severe pain who is nauseated and feels like he or she is about to vomit
A patient writhing and moaning from abdominal pain after abdominal surgery
correctAnswer: A patient who received morphine and has a pulse of 62 beats/min, respirations 10 breaths/min, and blood pressure 110/60 mm Hg

questionText: The nurse is preparing pain medications. To which patient does the nurse anticipate administering an opioid fentanyl patch?
options: A 15-year-old adolescent with a fractured femur
A 30-year-old adult with cellulitis
A 50-year-old patient with prostate cancer
An 80-year-old patient with a broken hip
correctAnswer: A 50-year-old patient with prostate cancer

questionText: A nurse is assessing a patient who started to have severe pain 3 days ago. When the nurse asks the patient to describe the pain, the patient states, “The pain feels like it is in my stomach. It is a burning pain, and it spreads out in a circle around the spot where it hurts the most.” Which type of pain does the nurse document the patient is having at this time?
options: Superficial Pain
Idiopathic Pain / Visceral Pain
Chronic Pain
correctAnswer: Idiopathic Pain / Visceral Pain

questionText: The nurse is evaluating the effectiveness of guided imagery for pain management as used for a patient who has second- and third-degree burns and needs extensive dressing changes. Which finding best indicates the effectiveness of guided imagery?
options: The patient’s facial expressions are stoic during the procedure.
The patient rates pain during the dressing change as a 6 on a scale of 0 to 10.
The patient’s need for analgesic medication decreases during the dressing changes.
The patient asks for pain medication during the dressing changes only once throughout the procedure.
correctAnswer: The patient’s need for analgesic medication decreases during the dressing changes.

questionText: A nurse is providing medication education to a patient who just started taking ibuprofen. Which information will the nurse include in the teaching session?
options: Ibuprofen helps to depress the central nervous system to decrease pain perception.
Ibuprofen reduces anxiety, which will help you cope with your pain.
Ibuprofen binds with opiate receptors to reduce your pain.
Ibuprofen inhibits the production of prostaglandins.
correctAnswer: Ibuprofen inhibits the production of prostaglandins.

questionText: A patient who had a motor vehicle crash 2 days ago is experiencing pain and is receiving patient-controlled analgesia (PCA). Which assessment finding indicates effective pain management with the PCA?
options: The patient is sleeping and is difficult to arouse.
The patient rates pain at a level of 2 on a 0 to 10 scale.
The patient has sufficient medication left in the PCA syringe.
The patient presses the control button to deliver pain medication.
correctAnswer: The patient rates pain at a level of 2 on a 0 to 10 scale.

questionText: A nurse receives an order from a health care provider to administer hydrocodone and acetaminophen (Vicodin ES 7.5/750), to a patient who is experiencing 8/10 postsurgical pain. The order is to give 2 tablets every 6 hours by mouth as needed for pain. What is the nurse’s next best action?
options: Give the Vicodin ES to the patient immediately because the patient is experiencing severe pain.
Ask the health care provider for a nonsteroidal antiinflammatory drug (NSAID) order.
Ask the health care provider to verify the dosage and frequency of the medication.
Give the Vicodin ES in addition to playing soothing music for the patient.
correctAnswer: Ask the health care provider to verify the dosage and frequency of the medication.

questionText: The nurse is administering ibuprofen (Advil) to an older patient. Which assessment data causes the nurse to hold the medication?
options: Patient states allergy to aspirin; Patient reports past medical history of gastric ulcer.
correctAnswer: Patient states allergy to aspirin; Patient reports past medical history of gastric ulcer.

questionText: A nurse is teaching a patient about patient-controlled analgesia (PCA). Which statement made by the patient indicates to the nurse that teaching is effective?
options: I will only need to be on this pain medication.
I feel less anxiety about the possibility of overdosing.
I can receive the pain medication as frequently as I need to.
I need the nurse to notify me when it is time for another dose.
correctAnswer: I feel less anxiety about the possibility of overdosing.

questionText: The nurse is administering pain medication for several patients. Which patient does the nurse administer medication to first?
options: The patient who needs to be premedicated before walking
The patient who has a PCA running that needs the syringe replaced
The patient who needs to take a scheduled dose of maintenance pain medication
The patient who is experiencing 8/10 pain and has an immediate order for pain medication
correctAnswer: The patient who is experiencing 8/10 pain and has an immediate order for pain medication

questionText: An oriented patient has recently had surgery. Which action is best for the nurse to take to assess this patient’s pain?
options: Assess the patient’s body language.
Ask the patient to rate the level of pain.
Observe the cardiac monitor for increased heart rate.
Have the patient describe the effect of pain on the ability to cope.
correctAnswer: Ask the patient to rate the level of pain.

questionText: A patient arrives at the emergency department experiencing a headache and rates the pain as 7 on a 0 to 10 pain scale. Which nonpharmacological intervention does the nurse implement for this patient while awaiting orders for pain medication from the health care provider?
options: Reassures the patient that the provider will come to the emergency department soon
Softly plays music that the patient finds relaxing
Frequently reassesses the patient’s pain scores
Teaches the patient how to do yoga
correctAnswer: Softly plays music that the patient finds relaxing

questionText: The nurse is caring for an infant in the intensive care unit. Which information should the nurse consider when planning care for this patient?
options: Infants cannot be assessed for pain.
Infants respond behaviorally and physiologically to painful stimuli.
Infants cannot tolerate analgesics owing to an underdeveloped metabolism.
Infants have a decreased sensitivity to pain when compared with older children.
correctAnswer: Infants respond behaviorally and physiologically to painful stimuli.

questionText: The nurse is assessing how a patient’s pain is affecting mobility. Which assessment question is most appropriate?
options: Have you considered working with a physical therapist?
What activities, if any, has your pain prevented you from doing?
Would you please rate your pain on a scale from 0 to 10 for me?
When does your pain medication typically take effect on your pain?
correctAnswer: What activities, if any, has your pain prevented you from doing?

questionText: A nurse is planning care for an older-adult patient who is experiencing pain. Which statement made by the nurse indicates the supervising nurse needs to follow up?
options: As adults age, their ability to perceive pain decreases.
Older patients may have low serum albumin in their blood, causing toxic effects of analgesic drugs.
Patients who have dementia probably experience pain, and their pain is not always well controlled.
It is safe to administer opioids to older adults as long as you start with small doses and frequently assess the patient’s response to the medication.
correctAnswer: As adults age, their ability to perceive pain decreases.

questionText: A nurse is caring for a patient who recently had abdominal surgery and is experiencing severe pain. The patient’s blood pressure is 110/60 mm Hg, and heart rate is 60 beats/min. Additionally, the patient does not appear to be in any distress. Which response by the nurse is most therapeutic?
options: Your vitals do not show that you are having pain; can you describe your pain?
OK, I will go get you some narcotic pain relievers immediately.
What would you like to try to alleviate your pain?
You do not look like you are in pain.
correctAnswer: What would you like to try to alleviate your pain?

questionText: A nurse is caring for a patient who is experiencing pain following abdominal surgery. Which information is important for the nurse to share with the patient when providing patient education about effective pain management?
options: To prevent overdose, you need to wait to ask for pain medication until you begin to experience pain.
You should take your medication after you walk to make sure you do not fall while you are walking.
We should work together to create a schedule to provide regular dosing of medication.
When you experience severe pain, you will need to take oral pain medications.
correctAnswer: We should work together to create a schedule to provide regular dosing of medication.

questionText: The nurse is assessing the characteristics of a patient’s pain. Match the characteristic to the question a nurse will ask to determine that specific characteristic.
options: Timing: How often does it recur?;  Location: Could you point to the area of pain?;  Severity: Could you rate your pain on a scale of 0 to 10?;  Aggravating Factors: Do certain activities worsen the pain?;  Quality: What does the pain feel like?
correctAnswer: Timing: How often does it recur?;  Location: Could you point to the area of pain?;  Severity: Could you rate your pain on a scale of 0 to 10?;  Aggravating Factors: Do certain activities worsen the pain?;  Quality: What does the pain feel like?

questionText: What is the primary reason for the importance of hand hygiene?
options: To prevent the spread of germs and reduce the risk of infection
To keep the hands clean and maintain personal hygiene
To improve the appearance and cleanliness of the hands
To comply with healthcare facility policies and regulations
correctAnswer: To prevent the spread of germs and reduce the risk of infection

questionText: A nurse is preparing to perform a complete bed bath and linen change on an occupied bed for an adult patient. Which of the following actions should the nurse avoid taking? (Select all that apply)
options: Cleaning the client's eyes from the inner to the outer canthus
Placing the bed in its lowest position to prevent client injury
Using CGH wipes on the client's face
Washing the patient's extremities from proximal to distal
Placing the client's dirty linens on the ground
correctAnswer: Placing the bed in its lowest position to prevent client injury

questionText: A client has been prescribed restraints for safety reasons. Which of the following actions should the nurse prioritize when applying restraints?
options: Offering food and fluids
Assessing skin integrity and providing skin care
Providing an opportunity for the client to use the bathroom
Providing range-of-motion exercises of extremities
correctAnswer: Assessing skin integrity and providing skin care

questionText: The nurse is assessing a client who has wrist restraints ordered. The client has a heart rate of 90 beats/minute and a respiratory rate of 20 breaths/minute. When looking at the client's wrist restraints, the nurse notices that the client's right hand is cold to the touch and appears blue-tinged. Based on the findings of (A), the nurse would immediately (B).
options: A cold and blue-tinged right hand; Remove the wrist restraint
correctAnswer: A cold and blue-tinged right hand; Remove the wrist restraint

questionText: What is an important dietary consideration for clients with immobility? (Select all that apply)
options: A high-fiber diet; A high-protein diet; Adequate fluid intake
A low-calorie diet; A high-sodium diet
correctAnswer: A high-fiber diet; A high-protein diet; Adequate fluid intake

questionText: The nurse is providing discharge education to a client about how to properly use a cane. The client will be using this cane after experiencing an injury of his right leg. Which statement indicates correct understanding?
options: I will hold my cane on my right side
I will move my cane 12 inches forward before taking a step
When walking with my cane, I will step my right leg up to the cane and then step my left leg past the cane
correctAnswer: When walking with my cane, I will step my right leg up to the cane and then step my left leg past the cane

questionText: A client with immobility is at risk for constipation. To promote bowel function in this client, the nurse should (A), which prevents constipation by (B).
options: encourage early ambulation; promoting bowel motility
correctAnswer: encourage early ambulation; promoting bowel motility

questionText: The nurse is teaching a new nurse on the unit about nasogastric (NG) tube feedings. Which of the following statements made by the new nurse requires additional teaching?
options: Every time before I use the NG tube, I will always confirm its placement with a chest x-ray
If I think the patient has aspirated, I will immediately stop the feeding
When initiating tube feedings, I will gradually increase the feeding rate based on the orders
The patient’s head of the bed should be kept between 30-45 degrees
correctAnswer: Every time before I use the NG tube, I will always confirm its placement with a chest x-ray

questionText: The nurse is administering Total Parenteral Nutrition (TPN) to a client. Select the 4 complications the client is at risk for developing as a result of receiving TPN:
options: Hyperglycemia; Hypoglycemia; Electrolyte imbalances; Infusion site infection
correctAnswer: Hyperglycemia; Hypoglycemia; Electrolyte imbalances; Infusion site infection

questionText: The nurse is caring for a client who is receiving an opioid pain medication and is expressing worry about developing tolerance and addiction to the pain medication. In one of their statements to the client, the nurse could explain that tolerance is (A), and addiction is (B).
options: The need for increased amounts of an opioid to achieve the same effect; The continued behavior pattern of using  an opioid despite its negative consequences
correctAnswer: The need for increased amounts of an opioid to achieve the same effect; The continued behavior pattern of using  an opioid despite its negative consequences

questionText: A nurse is caring for a group of clients with infections. Which infection is classified as a health care associated infection?
options: Respiratory infection contracted from a visitor
Vaginal infection in a postmenopausal woman
Urinary tract infection in a client who is sedentary
Wound infection caused by unwashed hands of a caregiver
correctAnswer: Wound infection caused by unwashed hands of a caregiver

questionText: A nurse is caring for a group of hospitalized clients. Which should the nurse do first to prevent client infections?
options: Provide small bedside bags to dispose of used tissues
Encourage staff to avoid coughing near clients
Administer antibiotics as prescribed
Identify clients at risk
correctAnswer: Identify clients at risk

questionText: A client's stool specimen is positive for Clostridium Difficile. Which isolation precautions should the nurse institute for this client?
options: Droplet
Contact
Reverse
Airborne
correctAnswer: Contact

questionText: Which should the nurse do to interrupt the transmission link in the chain of infection?
options: Wash the hands before providing care to a client
Position a commode next to a client's bed
Provide education about a balanced diet
Change a dressing when it is soiled
correctAnswer: Wash the hands before providing care to a client

questionText: From which type of isolation precaution is this mask designed to protect a nurse?
options: Contact
Airborne
Standard
Protective
correctAnswer: Airborne

questionText: The nurse is reviewing the clinical record of a newly admitted older adult male client. The client presents with fatigue, anorexia, fevers, burning on urination, urinary frequency, and cramping. Which piece of information should cause the most concern?
options: Temperature 103F
Abdominal cramping
WBC 30,000 cells/mcL
Blood Pressure 110/86 mmHg
correctAnswer: WBC 30,000 cells/mcL

questionText: A nurse must collect the following specimens. Which specimen does NOT require the use of surgical aseptic technique? Select all that apply.
options: Stool for occult blood; Stool for ova and parasites
correctAnswer: Stool for occult blood; Stool for ova and parasites

questionText: A client's urine is cloudy, amber, and has an unpleasant odor. Which problem may this information indicate that requires the nurse to make a focused assessment?
options: Urinary retention
Urinary tract infection
Ketone bodies in the urine
High urinary calcium level
correctAnswer: Urinary tract infection

questionText: Which of the following actions by the nurse comply with core principles of surgical asepsis? Select all that apply.
options: Keep the sterile field in view at all times; Consider the outer 1 inch of the sterile field as contaminated
correctAnswer: Keep the sterile field in view at all times; Consider the outer 1 inch of the sterile field as contaminated

questionText: What is the best way to minimize discomfort caused by the instillation of ear medication?
options: Warm the eardrops to room temperature before instillation.
Wear treatment gloves during the application process
Ask the patient to sit while introducing the medication.
Use a cotton-tipped applicator to remove any visible cerumen
correctAnswer: Warm the eardrops to room temperature before instillation.

questionText: Which instruction would help ensure the maximum therapeutic response when a patient self-administers ear medication?
options: Remain in the lateral position (unaffected side) for a few minutes after instillation
Bring refrigerated ear medication to room temperature before instillation.
Place a cotton ball firmly into the ear canal for 30 minutes after instillation.
Apply a warm, damp washcloth to the external ear to remove any crusted discharge.
correctAnswer: Remain in the lateral position (unaffected side) for a few minutes after instillation

questionText: Which statement or question best illustrates the nurse’s understanding of the role of nursing assistive personnel (NAP) in the instillation of ear medications?
options: Did you let the ear medication warm to room temperature?
Do you think the patient is capable of instilling her own eardrops?
Please tell the patient that the medication may make him dizzy when he stands up.
Be sure to keep the patient on her side for a few minutes, because I just administered her eardrops.
correctAnswer: Be sure to keep the patient on her side for a few minutes, because I just administered her eardrops.

questionText: After instructing a patient in the self-administration of antibiotic eardrops, what must come first in the nurse's assessment?
options: The patient's understanding of the medication's purpose
The patient's hand grasp, strength, coordination, and ability to manipulate the applicator
The patient's comprehension of the dosage instructions provided with the medication
The patient's ability to recognize the signs of an allergic reaction to the medication
correctAnswer: The patient's hand grasp, strength, coordination, and ability to manipulate the applicator

questionText: To ensure proper distribution of ear medication after instillation, what will the nurse instruct an adult patient to do?
options: Have a family member instill the medication.
Avoid contaminating the medication's applicator tip
Instill the medication at the time ordered by the provider.
Instill the medication after gently pulling the ear up and back.
correctAnswer: Instill the medication after gently pulling the ear up and back.

questionText: What is the best way for the nurse to minimize the risk of contaminating the patient's eye during the instillation of eye drops?
options: Encourage the patient to self-apply the medication.
Wear gloves during the entire application process
Introduce the medication onto the inner canthus of the eye.
Perform effective hand hygiene before and after the instillation.
correctAnswer: Perform effective hand hygiene before and after the instillation.

questionText: Which instruction should be given to a patient to ensure safety when self-applying and antibiotic ointment?
options: It is not necessary to allow refrigerated eye medication to warm to room temperature before administration.
Do not apply pressure directly to the eyeball when removing excess medication.
When cleaning the eye before administration, gently wash from the outer to the inner canthus.
Apply a warm, damp washcloth to the eye for several minutes to remove any crusted discharge.
correctAnswer: Do not apply pressure directly to the eyeball when removing excess medication.

questionText: Which statement or question best illustrates the nurse’s understanding of the role of nursing assistive personnel (NAP) in the instillation of eye medications?
options: Did you let the eye medication warm to room temperature?
Do you think the patient is capable of instilling his own eyedrops?
Be sure to slightly hyperextend her neck when instilling the medication.
Her vision may be temporarily impaired, so please help her to the bathroom.
correctAnswer: Her vision may be temporarily impaired, so please help her to the bathroom.

questionText: After instructing a patient in the self-administration of antibiotic eyedrops, what is the nurse's highest priority assessment?
options: The patient's understanding of the medication's purpose
The patient's hand grasp, strength, coordination, and ability to manipulate the applicator
The patient's comprehension of the dosage instructions provided with the medication
The patient's ability to recognize the signs of an allergic reaction to the medication
correctAnswer: The patient's hand grasp, strength, coordination, and ability to manipulate the applicator

questionText: When placing an intraocular disk, the nurse recognizes that it is in the correct position by assessing what?
options: Visibility of the disk over the cornea
Lack of visibility of the disk as it is placed under the lower eyelid
Lack of visibility as it is placed under the upper eyelid
Visibility of a small portion of the disk extending slightly above the lower eyelid
correctAnswer: Lack of visibility of the disk as it is placed under the lower eyelid

questionText: Which action by the nurse ensures patient safety when administering an intramuscular injection?
options: Putting on clean gloves before administration
Rotating injection sites
Aspirating for blood return when administering a vaccine
Injecting the medication quickly
correctAnswer: Rotating injection sites

questionText: When preparing an intramuscular injection, what can the nurse do to reduce the patient's risk for infection?
options: Wear clean gloves
Use a 3-mL syringe
Clean the injection site with an alcohol swab.
Massage the injection site
correctAnswer: Clean the injection site with an alcohol swab.

questionText: What can the nurse do to minimize the patient's risk for injury when delivering an intramuscular injection?
options: Instruct the patient to relax.
Insert the needle at a 45-degree angle.
Pull back on the plunger after inserting the needle.
Pull the skin taut at the injection site when inserting the needle.
correctAnswer: Pull back on the plunger after inserting the needle.

questionText: Which site is the preferred for intramuscular injections?
options: Ventrogluteal
Abdominal
Deltoid
Dorsogluteal
correctAnswer: Ventrogluteal

questionText: Which action by the nurse helps to ensure that the medication is delivered into the muscle when administering an intramuscular injection?
options: Using a 1-inch needle
Inserting the needle at a 45- to 60-degree angle
Withdrawing the needle immediately after delivering the medication
Aspirating for blood return before injecting the medication
correctAnswer: Aspirating for blood return before injecting the medication

questionText: How might the nurse safely administer an extended-release capsule to a patient with dysphagia?
options: Encourage the patient to drink plenty of water when swallowing the capsule.
Open the capsule, and place the contents into 90 mL (3 fl oz) of juice.
Place the capsule in a spoonful of the patient's applesauce.
Save the capsule to be administered last.
correctAnswer: Place the capsule in a spoonful of the patient's applesauce.

questionText: The nurse is preparing to administer several oral medications when the patient says he would like to take his pills with orange juice. What is the nurse's best response?
options: Determine whether the patient's prescribed diet includes orange juice.
Establish whether the medications may be taken with orange juice.
Ask the dietary aide to order extra orange juice for the unit.
Administer the pills with orange juice.
correctAnswer: Establish whether the medications may be taken with orange juice.

questionText: Which statement or question best illustrates the nurse's understanding of the role of nursing assistive personnel (NAP) in administering oral medications?
options: Does the patient need her pain medication?
Please make sure the patient has plenty of fresh water to take with her pills.
How much did the pain medication improve her pain?
Stay with the patient until he swallows all the pills.
correctAnswer: Please make sure the patient has plenty of fresh water to take with her pills.

questionText: The nurse has provided a patient with a prn oral analgesic that may be repeated as needed every 6 to 8 hours. What is the most appropriate follow-up action to ensure appropriate pain management?
options: Reassess the patient's pain in 30 to 40 minutes.
Document the patient's request for pain medication.
Administer the pain medication again in 6 hours.
Include the patient's pain history in the end-of-shift nursing report.
correctAnswer: Reassess the patient's pain in 30 to 40 minutes.

questionText: A patient with a history of nighttime confusion is to receive several oral medications at bedtime. What is the best way for the nurse to ensure that the patient has swallowed the medication?
options: Administer each tablet individually.
Observe the patient closely as he swallows the tablets.
Ask the patient to open his mouth after swallowing each tablet.
Ask the patient to swallow a full glass of water with the tablets.
correctAnswer: Ask the patient to open his mouth after swallowing each tablet.

questionText: How can the nurse best ensure the patient's safety when preparing insulin for administration?
options: Obtain the patient's current blood glucose level.
Clean the injection site with an antibacterial swab.
Apply clean gloves
Wipe the rubber seal of the vial with alcohol.
correctAnswer: Obtain the patient's current blood glucose level.

questionText: How would the nurse prepare insulin to ensure its efficacy?
options: Do not allow refrigerated insulin to warm up before administering it.
Follow aseptic technique during the entire process.
Roll the vial of insulin suspension between the palms prior to drawing up the medication.
Monitor the patient's blood glucose level before administering the injection.
correctAnswer: Roll the vial of insulin suspension between the palms prior to drawing up the medication.

questionText: When will a patient's blood glucose levels be most affected by a short-acting insulin injection, such as Humulin-R?
options: In 2 to 3 hours
For the next 12 hours
During unplanned exercise
When the patient eats carbohydrates
correctAnswer: In 2 to 3 hours

questionText: Which of the following statements is accurate regarding insulin administration?
options: Vials of insulin may be stored in the freezer to extend their shelf life.
If the rapid-acting insulin ordered is unavailable, it is safe to substitute an alternative rapid-acting insulin.
Vials of insulin must be inspected before each use for changes in appearance.
All insulin must be shaken before use to redistribute particles within the suspension.
correctAnswer: Vials of insulin must be inspected before each use for changes in appearance.

questionText: To prevent hypoglycemia and enhance efficacy, it is appropriate to give rapid-acting insulin how many minutes before the next meal?
options: 5 to 15 minutes
30 to 40 minutes
60 to 90 minutes
The timing of insulin around meals is not necessary.
correctAnswer: 5 to 15 minutes

questionText: A nurse is preparing to withdraw medication from an open multi dose vial. After confirming that the vial contains the appropriate medication and checking the expiration date, what would the nurse do next?
options: Apply clean gloves
Vigorously shake the vial
Wipe the rubber seal of the vial with an alcohol swab
Introduce air equal to the amount of medication needed.
correctAnswer: Wipe the rubber seal of the vial with an alcohol swab

questionText: What would the nurse do to remove air trapped in a syringe before withdrawing the syringe from the vial?
options: Position the tip of the needle in the vial's airspace, and tap the barrel of the syringe.
Position the tip of the needle below the fluid line, and tap the vial.
Position the vial on a flat surface, and tap the syringe.
Position the syringe above the vial, and tap the vial.
correctAnswer: Position the tip of the needle in the vial's airspace, and tap the barrel of the syringe.

questionText: How can the nurse prevent negative pressure from building up in the vial when preparing an injection?
options: Inject an amount of air into the vial equivalent to the volume of medication to be withdrawn.
Insert the needle through the center of the rubber seal.
Keep the tip of the needle below the level of fluid in the vial.
Tap the barrel of the syringe to dislodge air bubbles.
correctAnswer: Inject an amount of air into the vial equivalent to the volume of medication to be withdrawn.

questionText: How can the nurse ensure that medication from a single-dose vial is used appropriately?
options: Check to see when the medication vial was opened initially.
Write the date and his or her initials on the label when opening the vial.
Draw the entire amount of medication from the vial into the syringe.
Discard the vial and any remaining medication in the vial directly after use.
correctAnswer: Discard the vial and any remaining medication in the vial directly after use.

questionText: What will the nurse do after opening a multi dose vial and withdrawing a dose of medication from it?
options: Discard the unused portion of the medication.
Wipe the entire vial with an antiseptic swab.
Send the unused portion back to the pharmacy.
Label the vial with the date it was opened and your initials.
correctAnswer: Label the vial with the date it was opened and your initials.

questionText: When preparing an injection from an ampule, what will the nurse do if liquid is trapped in the neck of the ampule?
options: Check the medication cabinet for an extra ampule of the medication
Notify the pharmacy that an additional ampule of medication will be needed.
Use quick, light finger taps on the top of the ampule to move the liquid.
Shake the medication out of the neck of the ampule.
correctAnswer: Use quick, light finger taps on the top of the ampule to move the liquid.

questionText: What is the greatest safety concern when withdrawing medication from an ampule?
options: Not wearing gloves when preparing medication
Selecting an inappropriate needle size
Withdrawing glass particles into the syringe
Withdrawing bubbles into the syringe
correctAnswer: Withdrawing glass particles into the syringe

questionText: How does the nurse minimize the risk of patient infection when preparing medication from an ampule?
options: Using a filter needle to draw up the medication
Preparing the medication in the patient's room
Applying clean gloves while preparing the medication
Preserving the sterility of the needle while preparing the medication
correctAnswer: Preserving the sterility of the needle while preparing the medication

questionText: Which action minimizes the risk of introducing glass particles into the syringe when drawing medication from an ampule?
options: Using minimal force to snap the neck of the ampule
Using gauze to cover the top of the ampule when snapping it
Using a filter needle or straw to draw the medication from the ampule
Allowing the medication to settle after the ampule has been snapped open
correctAnswer: Using a filter needle or straw to draw the medication from the ampule

questionText: Which action might the nurse take when drawing up medication from an ampule?
options: Hold the ampule upside down while inserting the filter needle
Inject air into the ampule before withdrawing the medication
Hold the ampule horizontally while inserting the filter needle.
Expel air bubbles from the syringe while the filter needle is still inside the ampule
correctAnswer: Hold the ampule upside down while inserting the filter needle

questionText: When is a patient at a higher risk for a medication administration error?
options: During a care transition point, such as transfer to another unit
While on a hospital unit for an extended length of time
On the third postoperative day
When taking an active role in self-administration of insulin
correctAnswer: During a care transition point, such as transfer to another unit

questionText: As the nurse is giving a patient his medications, he remarks, 'I've never seen this blue pill before.' What is the nurse's correct response?
options: I'm sure the doctor knows what he's doing. Don't worry.
Our pharmacy probably sent a generic form of what you're used to taking.
What color pill are you used to seeing?
Don't take it. Let me double-check the doctor's order to make sure this is the correct medication for you.
correctAnswer: Don't take it. Let me double-check the doctor's order to make sure this is the correct medication for you.

questionText: What is the best way for the nurse to ensure that the patient does not receive the wrong dose because of a calculation error?
options: Ask the pharmacy to calculate the correct dosage.
Consult a current drug book to determine the new dosage.
Defer the calculation process to the provider.
Ask another registered nurse to verify the calculation.
correctAnswer: Ask another registered nurse to verify the calculation.

questionText: Which of the following nursing actions will reduce the risk of 'wrong route' when administering a medication?
options: Only splitting pills or tablets that have been prescored by the manufacturer
Using an oral dosing syringe when administering oral liquid medication
Transcribing a fractional dose of less than one with a leading zero (e.g., 0.5 mg)
Crushing an oral medication that is difficult to swallow
correctAnswer: Using an oral dosing syringe when administering oral liquid medication

questionText: What is the most appropriate way for the nurse to split an unscored tablet?
options: Use a pill-splitting device to split the unscored pill in half
Ask the pharmacy if it is appropriate to split the pill and if so, ask them to split and repackage it with the adjusted dose given on the label.
Use scissors to cut the pill in half.
Administer a whole pill every other day instead of every day.
correctAnswer: Ask the pharmacy if it is appropriate to split the pill and if so, ask them to split and repackage it with the adjusted dose given on the label.

questionText: To make sure the drug is delivered properly, what discharge instructions might the nurse give a patient who is being discharged with a dry powder inhaler (DPI)?
options: Rinse your mouth out with water after using the inhaler
Use the inhaler while sitting up in bed.
Keep track of the dosage using the counter on the inhaler.
After inhaling the medication, hold your breath for at least 10 seconds before exhaling.
correctAnswer: After inhaling the medication, hold your breath for at least 10 seconds before exhaling.

questionText: Which statement best illustrates the nurse's understanding of the role of nursing assistive personnel (NAP) in the use of a dry powder inhaler (DPI)?
options: Be sure to let me know if the patient starts coughing again.
Did you shake the inhaler well before giving it to the patient?
Do you think the patient is capable of using the inhaler independently?
Please tell the patient that the inhaler is to be used only when she is having trouble breathing.
correctAnswer: Be sure to let me know if the patient starts coughing again.

questionText: Before discharge, the nurse shows a patient how to use a dry powder inhaler (DPI). What should the nurse now assess?
options: Patient's understanding of the purpose of the medication
Patient's ability to handle, manipulate, and activate the DPI
Adequacy of the patient's planned daily medication schedule
Patient's awareness of the signs of an allergic reaction to the medication
correctAnswer: Patient's ability to handle, manipulate, and activate the DPI

questionText: When instructing a patient in the use of a dry powder inhaler (DPI), which statement is accurate?
options: It is important to shake the DPI before administering the medication.
It is important to exhale while the lips are still around mouthpiece.
It is important to read the manufacturer's instructions to determine how quickly to inhale the medication.
It is important for the patient to hold his or her breath for at least 60 seconds after inhaling the medication.
correctAnswer: It is important to read the manufacturer's instructions to determine how quickly to inhale the medication.

questionText: A nurse is preparing to help a patient administer a mucolytic agent using a metered-dose inhaler (MDI). What will the nurse do first in order to evaluate the medication's effectiveness?
options: Assess the patient's respiratory status before administration.
Warn against overuse of the inhaler
Discuss the side effects of the particular drug.
Verify the patient's identification according to agency policy.
correctAnswer: Assess the patient's respiratory status before administration.

questionText: Which discharge instruction would help to ensure that the patient achieves maximum therapeutic delivery of the medication when using a metered-dose inhaler (MDI)?
options: Make sure to report any adverse effects after using your inhaler.
Prime the inhaler if it is new or has not been used for several days.
Hold your breath for 60 seconds after the medication is delivered.
Use the inhaler while sitting up in a chair at 90-degree angle.
correctAnswer: Prime the inhaler if it is new or has not been used for several days.

questionText: Which statement best illustrates the nurse's understanding of the role of nursing assistive personnel (NAP) in the use of a metered-dose inhaler?
options: Be sure to let me know if she starts coughing again.
Show the patient how to clean the spacer chamber after she's finished with the inhaler.
Offer the patient her inhaler if it looks like she's short of breath.
Please tell her the inhaler is to be used no more than three times per day.
correctAnswer: Be sure to let me know if she starts coughing again.

questionText: A patient has been prescribed a metered-dose inhaler (MDI) containing 200 doses of a bronchodilator. The patient has been instructed to take two puffs of the medication three times daily. At this dosage, how long will the MDI last?
options: 100 days
50 days
66 days
33 days
correctAnswer: 33 days

questionText: The nurse is instructing a patient who is to receive both a bronchodilator and a steroid medication delivered by means of a metered-dose inhaler (MDI). Which instruction is necessary for the safe administration of both agents?
options: Make sure to use the steroid medication before the bronchodilator.
Make sure to use the bronchodilator before the steroid medication.
Rinse your mouth with warm water before using the MDI to administer either medication.
Make sure you wait at least 30 seconds between administering the bronchodilator and administering the steroid medication.
correctAnswer: Make sure to use the bronchodilator before the steroid medication.

questionText: What is the nurse's first step in preparing to administer a prescribed medication using an automated medication dispensing system?
options: Establish the patient's ID using two identifiers.
Review the medication administration record (MAR).
Provide patient education
Review applicable nursing considerations.
correctAnswer: Review the medication administration record (MAR).

questionText: Which precaution should the nurse observe to ensure patient safety when using an automated medication dispensing system?
options: Assess the patient's ability to swallow oral medications without difficulty.
Ask the patient about his or her experience with this or similar medications.
Prepare medications for one patient at a time.
Evaluate the patient's understanding of the safety issues related to the specific drug.
correctAnswer: Prepare medications for one patient at a time.

questionText: Which statement or question best illustrates the nurse's understanding of the role of nursing assistive personnel (NAP) in using automated medication dispensing systems?
options: Does the patient need her pain medication?
Let me know if she complains of any nausea.
Ask her to describe her pain and show you where it is.
Remember to log off of the system when you are finished.
correctAnswer: Let me know if she complains of any nausea.

questionText: Which action by the nurse is most important in protecting the safety of patients and staff when using an automated medication dispensing system?
options: Refusing to share his or her individual security log-in code for the dispensing system
Having another registered nurse check his or her mathematical calculations
Reviewing a current drug book for dosing information
Using two different mathematical formulas to cross-check a dosage calculation
correctAnswer: Refusing to share his or her individual security log-in code for the dispensing system

questionText: While preparing a patient's oral medication dispensed from an automated medication dispensing system, the nurse realizes that the pill dispensed is twice the correct dose. What is the nurse's best action at this time?
options: Notify the health care provider and ask if the higher dose could be given.
Access the dispenser again for the correct dose.
Notify the pharmacy to determine if the accurate dose is available.
Split the pill in half.
correctAnswer: Notify the pharmacy to determine if the accurate dose is available.

questionText: A client has a wound that is healing by secondary intention. Which solution to cleanse the wound and dressing should the nurse expect will be prescribed to support wound healing?
options: Normal saline and a gauze dressing
Normal saline and a wet-to-damp dressing
Povidone-iodine and a dry sterile dressing
Half peroxide and half normal saline and a wet-to-dry dressing
correctAnswer: Normal saline and a wet-to-damp dressing

questionText: A nurse is caring for a group of clients experiencing various medical conditions. Which condition places the client at the highest risk for a wound infection?
options: Surgical creation of a colostomy
First-degree burn on the back
Puncture of the foot by a nail
Paper cut on the finger
correctAnswer: Puncture of the foot by a nail

questionText: A nurse is caring for clients with a variety of wounds. Which wound will likely heal by primary intention? Select all that apply.
options: Cut in the skin from a knife; Surgical incision
Excoriated perianal area; Abrasion of the skin; Pressure ulcer
correctAnswer: Cut in the skin from a knife; Surgical incision

questionText: A client has a wound infection. which local human response should the nurse expect to identify? Select all that apply.
options: Leukocytosis
Malaise; Fever
Edema; Pain
correctAnswer: Edema; Pain

questionText: A nurse plans to remove a client's wound dressing. The nurse identifies the client, provides for the client's privacy, and places the client in an appropriate and comfortable position. Place the following steps in the order in which they should be implemented when removing the soiled dressing.
options: Don clean gloves; Loosen the edges of the tape around the dressing starting from the outside and moving toward the cente; Pull the tape away from the skin gently; Remove the dressing by lifting the edge of the dressing upward and toward the center of the wound; Assess the volume/color/and odor of exudate; Place the soiled dressing and gloves in a biohazardous waste receptacle
correctAnswer: Don clean gloves; Loosen the edges of the tape around the dressing starting from the outside and moving toward the cente; Pull the tape away from the skin gently; Remove the dressing by lifting the edge of the dressing upward and toward the center of the wound; Assess the volume/color/and odor of exudate; Place the soiled dressing and gloves in a biohazardous waste receptacle

Not a pressure injury

options: Stage 2
Stage 3

questionText: Which term best describes the wound bed in the second picture?
options: Macerated tissue
Granulation tissue
Serous tissue

Mechanical

questionText: ___ is the type of debridement utilized when Santyl is applied to a wound (as shown in the picture below).

questionText: Which of the following wounds would you expect be ordered to heal by secondary intention?
options: Surgical incision on the ankle
Clean cut caused by a clean knife
Pressure injury of the sacrum

questionText: Which of the following patients is most at risk for developing skin breakdown?
options: 30 year old female, Dx: IBS, up ad lib
58 year old male, Dx: colon CA, up with standby assist
5 year old female, Dx: Tonsillitis s/p T&A
72 year old male, Dx: Stoke, bedrest/unable to ambulate
correctAnswer: 72 year old male, Dx: Stoke, bedrest/unable to ambulate

questionText: A nurse is caring for a debilitated female client with nocturia. Which nursing intervention is the priority when planning to meet this client's needs?
options: Encouraging the use of bladder training exercises
Providing assistance with toileting every 4 hours
Positioning a bedside commode near the bed
Teaching the avoidance of fluids after 5 p.m.
correctAnswer: Positioning a bedside commode near the bed

questionText: A nurse reviews the results of a client's urinalysis. Which constituent found in urine indicates the presence of an abnormality that should be reported to the health care provider?
options: Electrolytes
Protein
Water
Urea
correctAnswer: Protein

questionText: A nurse is performing a physical assessment on a newly admitted client who is experiencing urinary incontinence. Which problem identified by the nurse is often associated with this problem?
options: Chronic pain
Reduced fluid intake
Disturbed self-esteem
Insufficient knowledge
correctAnswer: Disturbed self-esteem

questionText: Which is an effective nursing intervention to prevent urinary tract infections?
options: Teach female clients to wipe from the back to the front after urinating
Advise clients to report burning on urination to health-care providers
Instruct clients to use bath powder to absorb perineal perspiration
Encourage clients to drink several quarts of fluid daily
correctAnswer: Encourage clients to drink several quarts of fluid daily

questionText: A client has urinary incontinence. Which is the best nursing intervention for this client?
options: Providing skin care immediately after soiling
Using a deodorant soap when providing skin care
Drying the area well after providing perineal care
Dusting the perineal area with a light film of cornstarch
correctAnswer: Providing skin care immediately after soiling

questionText: A client has a urinary catheter. Which is most important when the nurse cares for this client?
options: Applying an antimicrobial agent to the urinary meatus 2 times a day
Ensuring that the catheter remains connected to the collection bag
Wearing sterile gloves when accessing the specimen port
Increasing fluid intake to 3,000mL a day
correctAnswer: Ensuring that the catheter remains connected to the collection bag

questionText: A nurse is inserting an indwelling urinary catheter into a male client. The nurse feels firm resistance while inserting the catheter into the penis. What should the nurse do?
options: Lower the penis until it is parallel to the length of the body
Inflate the balloon of the catheter with 10mL of normal saline
Stop the procedure and notify the health-care provider about the resistance
Use a twisting motion and firmly advance the catheter 2 inches farther into the penis
correctAnswer: Stop the procedure and notify the health-care provider about the resistance

questionText: A nurse is caring for a client with a condom catheter. Which of the following is important to implement? Select all that apply.
options: Avoiding kinks in the collection tubing; Providing perineal care at least once a day; Confirming that the adhesive band is not too tight; Leaving 1 inch between the glans penis and the drainage tubing; Ensuring that the foreskin is over the glans penis before the catheter is applies
correctAnswer: Avoiding kinks in the collection tubing; Providing perineal care at least once a day; Confirming that the adhesive band is not too tight; Leaving 1 inch between the glans penis and the drainage tubing; Ensuring that the foreskin is over the glans penis before the catheter is applies

questionText: A nurse is caring for a female client on bedrest who has a urinary catheter. Which should the nurse do? Select all that apply.
options: Position the tubing through the side rail of the bed
Ensure the tubing is positioned under the leg
Label the tubing with the date of insertion
Irrigate the tubing to ensure its patency
Secure the tubing to the client's leg
correctAnswer: Secure the tubing to the client's leg

questionText: Which should the nurse implement to facilitate bladder continence for a male client who is cognitively impaired? Select all that apply.
options: Offer toileting reminders every 2 hours; Providing clothing that is easy to manipulate
Apply a condom catheter in the morning; Encourage avoidance of fluids between meals; Explain the need to call for help with toileting every 4 hours
correctAnswer: Offer toileting reminders every 2 hours; Providing clothing that is easy to manipulate

questionText: A nurse must obtain a urine specimen for a culture and sensitivity test from a client who has an indwelling urinary catheter. Place the following steps in the order in which they should be performed.
options: While wearing clean gloves drain the urine in the tubing into the drainage bag; Clamp the drainage tubing below the specimen port for 15-30 minutes; Wash your hands and don clean gloves; Swab the specimen port with an antiseptic and aspirate urine via a sterile syringe; Transfer the urine to a sterile specimen cup and discard the syringe into the appropriate container; Remove the clamp from the drainage tubing
correctAnswer: While wearing clean gloves drain the urine in the tubing into the drainage bag; Clamp the drainage tubing below the specimen port for 15-30 minutes; Wash your hands and don clean gloves; Swab the specimen port with an antiseptic and aspirate urine via a sterile syringe; Transfer the urine to a sterile specimen cup and discard the syringe into the appropriate container; Remove the clamp from the drainage tubing

questionText: Which of the following is the primary function of the kidney?
options: Metabolizing and excreting medications
Maintaining fluid and electrolyte balance
Storing and excreting urine
Filtering blood cells and proteins
correctAnswer: Maintaining fluid and electrolyte balance

questionText: An 86-year-old patient tells the nurse that she is experiencing uncontrollable leakage of urine. Which nursing diagnosis should the nurse include in the patient’s plan of care?
options: Urinary retention
Urinary hesitancy
Urinary urgency
Urinary incontinence
correctAnswer: Urinary incontinence

questionText: To obtain a clean-voided urine specimen for a female patient, the nurse should teach the patient to
options: Cleanse the urethral meatus from the area of most contamination to least
Initiate the first part of the urine stream directly into the collection cup
Hold the labia apart while voiding into the specimen cup
Drink fluids 5 minutes before collecting the urine specimen
correctAnswer: Hold the labia apart while voiding into the specimen cup

questionText: The nurse would expect the urine of a patient with uncontrolled diabetes mellitus to be
options: Cloudy
Discolored
Sweet smelling
Painful
correctAnswer: Sweet smelling

questionText: Many individuals have difficulty voiding in a bedpan or urinal while lying in bed because they
options: Are embarrassed that they will urinate on the bedding
Would feel more comfortable assuming a normal voiding position
Feel they are losing their independence by asking the nursing staff to help
Are worried about acquiring a urinary tract infection
correctAnswer: Would feel more comfortable assuming a normal voiding position

questionText: When caring for a hospitalized patient with a urinary catheter, which nursing action best prevents the patient from acquiring an infection?
options: Inserting the catheter using strict clean technique
Performing hand hygiene before and after providing perineal care
Fully inflating the catheter’s balloon according to the manufacturer’s recommendation
Disconnecting and replacing the catheter drainage bag once per shift
correctAnswer: Performing hand hygiene before and after providing perineal care

questionText: A patient requests the nurse’s assistance to the bedside commode and becomes frustrated when unable to void in front of the nurse. The nurse understands the patient’s inability to void because
options: Anxiety can make it difficult for abdominal and perineal muscles to relax enough to void
The patient does not recognize the physiological signals that indicate a need to void.
The patient is lonely, and calling the nurse in under false pretenses is a way to get attention
The patient is not drinking enough fluids to produce adequate urine output
correctAnswer: Anxiety can make it difficult for abdominal and perineal muscles to relax enough to void

questionText: What signs and symptoms would the nurse expect to observe in a patient with excessive white blood cells present in the urine?
options: Fever and chills
Difficulty holding urine
Increased blood pressure
Abnormal blood sugar
correctAnswer: Fever and chills

questionText: The nurse knows that which indwelling catheter procedure places the patient at greatest risk for acquiring a urinary tract infection?
options: Emptying the drainage bag every 8 hours or when half full
Kinking the catheter tubing to obtain a urine specimen
Placing the drainage bag on the side rail of the patient’s bed
Failing to secure the catheter tubing to the patient’s thigh
correctAnswer: Placing the drainage bag on the side rail of the patient’s bed

questionText: The nurse would question an order to insert a urinary catheter on which patient?
options: A 26-year-old patient with a recent spinal cord injury at T2
A 30-year-old patient requiring drug screening for employment
A 40-year-old patient undergoing bladder repair surgery
An 86-year-old patient requiring monitoring of urinary output for renal failure
correctAnswer: A 30-year-old patient requiring drug screening for employment

questionText: Which nursing actions are acceptable when collecting a urine specimen? (Select all that apply.)
options: Growing urine cultures for up to 12 hours; Wearing gown, gloves, and mask for all specimen handling; Squeezing urine from diapers into a urine specimen cup

Labeling all specimens with date, time, and initials; Allowing the patient adequate time and privacy to void; Transporting specimens to the laboratory in a timely fashion; Placing a plastic bag over the child’s urethra to catch urine
correctAnswer: Labeling all specimens with date, time, and initials; Allowing the patient adequate time and privacy to void; Transporting specimens to the laboratory in a timely fashion; Placing a plastic bag over the child’s urethra to catch urine

questionText: Which of the following are indications for irrigating a urinary catheter? (Select all that apply.)
options: Sediment occluding within the tubing; Blood clots in the bladder following surgery; Bladder infection
Rupture of the catheter balloon; Presence of renal calculi
correctAnswer: Sediment occluding within the tubing; Blood clots in the bladder following surgery; Bladder infection

questionText: Upon palpation, the nurse notices that the bladder is firm and distended; the patient expresses an urge to urinate. The nurse should follow up by asking
options: When was the last time you voided?
Do you lose urine when you cough or sneeze?
Have you noticed any change in your urination patterns?
Do you have a fever or chills?
correctAnswer: When was the last time you voided?

questionText: A patient asks about treatment for urge urinary incontinence. The nurse’s best response is to advise the patient to
options: Perform pelvic floor exercises
Drink cranberry juice
Avoid voiding frequently
Wear an adult diaper
correctAnswer: Perform pelvic floor exercises

questionText: A patient is having difficulty voiding in a bedpan but states that she feels her bladder is full. To stimulation micturition, which nursing intervention should the nurse try first?
options: Exiting the room and informing the patient that the nurse will return in 30 minutes to check on the patient’s progress
Utilizing the power of suggestion by turning on the faucet and letting the water run
Obtaining an order for a Foley catheter
Administering diuretic medication
correctAnswer: Utilizing the power of suggestion by turning on the faucet and letting the water run

questionText: A nurse notifies the provider immediately if a patient with an indwelling catheter
options: Complains of discomfort upon insertion of the catheter
Places the drainage bag higher than the waist while ambulating
Has not collected any urine in the drainage bag for 2 hours
Is incontinent of stool and contaminates the external portion of the catheter
correctAnswer: Has not collected any urine in the drainage bag for 2 hours

questionText: A nurse is caring for a client who is experiencing diarrhea. Which physiological response to diarrhea should the nurse be most concerned about?
options: Dehydration
Malnutrition
Excoriated skin
Urinary incontinence
correctAnswer: Dehydration

questionText: A nurse identifies that a client's colostomy stoma is pale. Which should the nurse do?
options: Notify the surgeon
Listen for bowel sounds
Wash the area with warm water
Gently massage around the stoma
correctAnswer: Notify the surgeon

questionText: A nurse is teaching a client with a history of constipation about the excessive use of laxatives. Which effect of laxatives should the nurse include as the primary reason why their use should be avoided?
options: Weakens the natural response to defecation
Results in distention of the intestines
Causes abdominal discomfort
Precipitates incontinence
correctAnswer: Weakens the natural response to defecation

questionText: A nurse identifies that a client has tarry stools. Which problem should the nurse conclude that the client is experiencing?
options: Upper GI bleeding
Pancreatic dysfunction
Lactulose intolerance
Inadequate bile salts?
correctAnswer: Upper GI bleeding

questionText: A nurse is teaching a client how to irrigate a colostomy. The client asks, 'Why is it necessary to use the cone attachment to the irrigation catheter?' What information should the nurse include in a response to this question?
options: Stops enema solution from flowing out of the bowel during the procedure
Prevents prolapse of the bowel during evacuation of the solution
Dilates the stoma so that the enema tube can be inserted
Faciliates the elimination of drainage from the colon
correctAnswer: Stops enema solution from flowing out of the bowel during the procedure

questionText: Which outcome of the options presented is most appropriate for a client with perceived constipation?
options: Have a bowel movement without the use of a laxative
Explain the rationale for the use of laxatives
Drink 8 glasses of water per day
Defecate every day
correctAnswer: Have a bowel movement without the use of a laxative

questionText: A primary health-care provider prescribes a tap-water enema for a client. The client asks about the purpose of the enema. Which specific information about the purpose of a tap-water enema should be included in the nurse's response?
options: It reduces abdominal gas
It drains the urinary bladder
It empties the bowel of stool
It limits nausea and vomiting
correctAnswer: It empties the bowel of stool

questionText: Which word is specific regarding how a soapsuds enema works on the mucosa of the bowel?
options: Dilating
Irritating
Softening
Lubricating
correctAnswer: Irritating

questionText: A nurse is caring for a client with an intestinal stoma. Which intervention is most important?
options: Cleansing the stoma with cool water
Spraying an air-freshening deodorant in the room
Selecting a bag with an appropriate-size stomal opening
Wearing sterile, non-latex gloves when caring for the stoma
correctAnswer: Selecting a bag with an appropriate-size stomal opening

questionText: The nurse knows that most nutrients are absorbed in which portion of the digestive tract?
options: Stomach
Duodenum
Ileum
Cecum
correctAnswer: Duodenum

questionText: The nurse would expect the least formed stool to be present in which portion of the digestive tract?
options: Ascending
Descending
Transverse
Sigmoid
correctAnswer: Ascending

questionText: Which of the following is NOT a function of the large intestine?
options: Absorbing nutrients
Absorbing water
Secreting Bicarbonate
Eliminating Waste
correctAnswer: Absorbing nutrients

questionText: Fecal impactions occur in which portion of the colon?
options: Ascending
Descending
Transverse
Rectum
correctAnswer: Rectum

questionText: The nurse is caring for a patient who is confined to the bed. The nurse asks the patient if he needs to have a bowel movement 30 minutes after eating a meal because
options: The digested food needs to make room for recently ingested food
Mastication triggers the digestive system to begin peristalsis
The smell of bowel elimination in the room would deter the patient from eating
More ancillary staff members are available after meal times
correctAnswer: Mastication triggers the digestive system to begin peristalsis

questionText: The nurse should place the patient in which position when preparing to administer an enema?
options: Left sims
Fowlers
Supine
Semi-Fowlers
correctAnswer: Left sims

questionText: A nurse is performing an assessment on a patient who has not had a bowel movement in 3 days. The nurse would expect which other assessment finding?
options: Hypoactive bowel sounds
Jaundice in sclera
Decreased skin turgor
Soft, tender abdomen
correctAnswer: Hypoactive bowel sounds

questionText: Which nursing intervention is most effective in promoting normal defecation for a patient who has muscle weakness in the legs that prevents ambulation?
options: Elevate the head of the bed 45 degrees 60 minutes after breakfast
Use a mobility device to place the patient on a bedside commode
Give the patient a pillow to brace against the abdomen while bearing down
Administer a soap suds enema every 2 hours
